tokens; admin user is created or updated in DB; admin is added as OWNER to all servers |\n-| Disable admin override in production | `NODE_ENV = 'production'`, email: `\"admin@harmony.dev\"`, password: `\"admin\"` | Throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` (normal credential check applies, admin user likely does not exist) |\n+| Login with valid credentials | email: `\"user@example.com\"`, passwordVerifier: `<correct verifier>` (user exists with matching hash) | Returns `{ accessToken, refreshToken }`; both are non-empty and JWT-signed; refresh token is stored in DB |\n+| Reject login with wrong verifier | email: `\"user@example.com\"` (exists), passwordVerifier: `<wrong verifier>` | Throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` and message `\"Invalid credentials\"` |\n+| Reject login for non-existent email | email: `\"nonexistent@example.com\"`, passwordVerifier: `<any verifier>` | Throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` and message `\"Invalid credentials\"`; timing-safe dummy hash check is performed |\n+| Timing-safe bcrypt comparison on non-existent email | Non-existent email + any verifier | Spy on `bcrypt.compare()` verifies it is called with (passwordVerifier, TIMING_DUMMY_HASH) even though user lookup failed (prevents timing-based user enumeration) |\n+| Reject legacy bcrypt-only credential records | email exists, `passwordHash` lacks the `v1$<salt>$...` prefix | Throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` and message instructing a password reset |\n+| Admin override in non-production | `NODE_ENV = 'development'`, email: `\"admin@harmony.dev\"`, passwordVerifier derived from `\"admin\"` and the dev-admin salt | Returns valid tokens; admin user is created or updated in DB; admin is added as OWNER to all servers |\n+| Disable admin override in production | `NODE_ENV = 'production'`, email: `\"admin@harmony.dev\"`, verifier derived from `\"admin\"` | Throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` (normal credential check applies, admin user likely does not exist) |\n | Admin override creates user if not exists | `NODE_ENV = 'development'`, admin email provided | User is created with `email = ADMIN_EMAIL`, `username = 'admin'`, `displayName = 'System Admin'` |\n | Admin override makes admin OWNER of all servers | `NODE_ENV = 'development'`, admin login, 3+ servers exist | For each server in the database, a serverMember record is created or updated with role `OWNER` |\n | Admin user creation fails on DB error | `NODE_ENV = 'development'`, `admin@harmony.dev`/`admin`, but `prisma.user.upsert()` throws error | Throws error (does not silently fail); login fails |\n@@ -206,11 +230,20 @@ Description: validates an access token and returns the decoded payload (pure ver\n | No database interaction | Any token | Function does not call any database methods; verification is pure |\n | Reject malformed token | accessToken: `\"not.a.jwt\"`, `\"invalid\"`, or `\"\"` | Throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` |\n \n+### 4.0 Helper coverage\n+\n+| Test Purpose | Inputs | Expected Output |\n+|---|---|---|\n+| Generate registration salt | none | Returns a 32-character hexadecimal string |\n+| Return stored login salt | email for existing user with `v1$<salt>$...` password hash | Returns the embedded `passwordSalt` |\n+| Return deterministic dummy login salt | unknown email, same input repeated twice | Returns the same 32-character hexadecimal string both times |\n+\n ## 5. Edge Cases to Explicitly Validate\n \n ### Security & Timing\n - **Timing attacks on login**: ensure `bcrypt.compare()` is ALWAYS called for non-existent emails, even though the user lookup fails early. Spy on bcrypt to verify this.\n - **No user enumeration**: both \"user not found\" and \"wrong password\" return identical error message and timing.\n+- **No raw password transport in service contract**: callers must derive and submit `passwordVerifier`; service tests should not pass raw passwords into `register` or `login`.\n - **JWT secret enforcement**: tokens signed with a different secret are rejected. Note: `jsonwebtoken` does not enforce an algorithm allowlist by default, so algorithm mismatch tests (e.g., HS512 vs HS256) may not fail with a shared HMAC secret.\n - **No plaintext password storage**: verify password is never logged, returned, or stored unencrypted.\n \n@@ -18,7 +18,7 @@ Each feature spec was authored independently and defines its own modules, classe\n | **Database** | PostgreSQL 16+ | ACID guarantees for visibility state transitions; native `ENUM` types for visibility; `JSONB` for flexible audit payloads; partial indexes for efficient public-channel queries. |\n | **Cache / EventBus** | Redis 7.2+ | Sub-millisecond reads for visibility checks on every public page load; Pub/Sub for cross-module event propagation (`VISIBILITY_CHANGED`, `MESSAGE_CREATED`, etc.) without tight coupling. |\n | **Authenticated APIs** | tRPC 11 | End-to-end type inference between Next.js client and Express server; eliminates hand-written API clients for admin operations. |\n-| **Public APIs** | REST (Express) | Search-engine crawlers, social-media link unfurlers, and external consumers require plain HTTP. tRPC's binary protocol is invisible to these consumers. |\n+| **Public APIs** | REST (Express) | Search-engine crawlers, social-media link unfurlers, and external consumers require plain HTTP. tRPC's binary protocol is invisible to these consumers. Authentication also uses REST challenge endpoints so the browser can fetch salts and submit password verifiers without sending raw passwords in request bodies. |\n | **Real-time Transport** | SSE (Server-Sent Events) | Native browser `EventSource` requires no client library; unidirectional server→client push fits message delivery perfectly; works over existing HTTP infrastructure with no WebSocket upgrade negotiation. |\n | **ORM** | Prisma 5.8+ | Type-safe schema definitions; auto-generated migrations; integrates with PostgreSQL enums. |\n | **Runtime Validation** | Zod 3.22+ | Composes with tRPC for automatic request/response validation; shared between client and server. |\n@@ -44,6 +44,8 @@ Each feature spec was authored independently and defines its own modules, classe\n \n **Why the split?** Crawlers (Googlebot, Bingbot) and social-media unfurlers (Facebook, Twitter/X, Slack) make standard HTTP requests. They cannot consume tRPC. Admin operations (visibility toggling, meta-tag overrides) benefit from tRPC's type inference and are only used by authenticated Harmony clients.\n \n+For authentication specifically, the REST surface also includes `/api/auth/register/challenge` and `/api/auth/login/challenge`. The browser uses these endpoints to fetch salts, derive a PBKDF2-SHA256 `passwordVerifier` locally, and then submit the verifier to `/api/auth/register` or `/api/auth/login` instead of the raw password.\n+\n ---\n \n ## 2. System Architecture Overview\n@@ -122,7 +124,7 @@ The `M-B`/`M-D` IDs are architectural groupings (by technical role). The detaile\n | *M-CV2* | *Public Channel Viewer* | *Client* | *Channel Visibility Toggle* | *Specified in [channel visibility spec](./dev-spec-channel-visibility-toggle.md)* | *—* |\n | *M-GV1* | *Public View (SSR)* | *Client* | *Guest Public Channel View* | *Specified in [guest public channel spec](./dev-spec-guest-public-channel-view.md)* | *—* |\n | *M-GV2* | *Client Interaction* | *Client* | *Guest Public Channel View* | *Specified in [guest public channel spec](./dev-spec-guest-public-channel-view.md)* | *—* |\n-| M-B1 | API Gateway | Server | Shared | tRPC router (authenticated) + REST controllers (public) | Cross-cutting infrastructure (all modules) |\n+| M-B1 | API Gateway | Server | Shared | tRPC router (authenticated) + REST controllers (public and auth challenge endpoints) | Cross-cutting infrastructure (all modules) |\n | M-B2 | Access Control | Server | Shared | Visibility guard, content filter, rate limiter, anonymous sessions | Module 1 (Auth middleware), Module 11 (Permission System) |\n | M-B3 | Visibility Management | Server | Channel Visibility Toggle | Visibility state machine, permission checks, audit logging | Module 3 (Channel Management — visibility ops) |\n | M-B4 | Content Delivery | Server | Guest Public Channel View | Message retrieval, author privacy, attachment processing | Module 9 (Public API & SEO — public delivery) |\n@@ -166,6 +166,11 @@ npx prisma migrate deploy\n # 4. expect(mockedApiClient.post).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(2, '/api/auth/login', {\n+      email: 'user@example.com',\n+      passwordVerifier: 'derived-password-verifier',\n+    });\n+    expect(mockedApiClient.post).not.toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(2, '/api/auth/login', {\n+      email: 'user@example.com',\n+      password: 'plain-text-password',\n+    });\n+    expect(mockedSetTokens).toHaveBeenCalledWith('access', 'refresh');\n+  });\n+\n+  it('requests a registration salt and never posts the raw password during signup', async () => {\n+    mockedApiClient.post\n+      .mockResolvedValueOnce({ passwordSalt: 'ffeeddccbbaa99887766554433221100' })\n+      .mockResolvedValueOnce({ accessToken: 'access', refreshToken: 'refresh' });\n+\n+    await register('user@example.com', 'alice', 'Alice', 'plain-text-password');\n+\n+    expect(mockedApiClient.post).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(1, '/api/auth/register/challenge');\n+    expect(mockedDerivePasswordVerifier).toHaveBeenCalledWith(\n+      'plain-text-password',\n+      'ffeeddccbbaa99887766554433221100',\n+    );\n+    expect(mockedApiClient.post).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(2, '/api/auth/register', {\n+      email: 'user@example.com',\n+      username: 'alice',\n+      passwordSalt: 'ffeeddccbbaa99887766554433221100',\n+      passwordVerifier: 'derived-password-verifier',\n+    });\n+    expect(mockedApiClient.post).not.toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(2, '/api/auth/register', {\n+      email: 'user@example.com',\n+      username: 'alice',\n+      password: 'plain-text-password',\n+    });\n+  });\n+});\n@@ -210,9 +210,9 @@ describe('Fix 3 — BrowseServersModal: onJoined prop is called on successful jo\n     const callOrder: string[] = [];\n     const serverId = 'srv-abc';\n \n-    const onJoined = jest.fn(() => callOrder.push('onJoined'));\n-    const onClose = jest.fn(() => callOrder.push('onClose'));\n-    const routerPush = jest.fn(() => callOrder.push('routerPush'));\n+    const onJoined = jest.fn<void, [string]>(_id => callOrder.push('onJoined'));\n+    const onClose = jest.fn<void, []>(() => callOrder.push('onClose'));\n+    const routerPush = jest.fn<void, [string]>(_href => callOrder.push('routerPush'));\n \n     // Replicate the success path of handleJoin in BrowseServersModal\n     onJoined(serverId);\n@@ -224,12 +224,16 @@ describe('Fix 3 — BrowseServersModal: onJoined prop is called on successful jo\n   });\n \n   it('onJoined is optional — omitting it does not throw', () => {\n-    const onJoined: ((id: string) => void) | undefined = undefined;\n-    expect(() => onJoined?.('srv-xyz')).not.toThrow();\n+    let onJoined: ((id: string) => void) | undefined;\n+    expect(() => {\n+      if (onJoined) {\n+        onJoined('srv-xyz');\n+      }\n+    }).not.toThrow();\n   });\n \n   it('onJoined is called exactly once per successful join', () => {\n-    const onJoined = jest.fn();\n+    const onJoined = jest.fn<void, [string]>();\n     onJoined('srv-123');\n     expect(onJoined).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);\n     expect(onJoined).toHaveBeenCalledWith('srv-123');\n@@ -0,0 +1,58 @@\n+const PASSWORD_VERIFIER_ITERATIONS = 310000;\n+const PASSWORD_VERIFIER_LENGTH = 32;\n+\n+function decodeHex(hex: string): ArrayBuffer {\n+  if (hex.length % 2 !== 0) {\n+    throw new Error('Invalid password salt');\n+  }\n+\n+  const bytes = new Uint8Array(hex.length / 2);\n+  for (let index = 0; index < hex.length; index += 2) {\n+    bytes[index / 2] = Number.parseInt(hex.slice(index, index + 2), 16);\n+  }\n+\n+  return bytes.buffer.slice(bytes.byteOffset, bytes.byteOffset + bytes.byteLength);\n+}\n+\n+function encodeBase64(bytes: Uint8Array): string {\n+  if (typeof window === 'undefined') {\n+    return Buffer.from(bytes).toString('base64');\n+  }\n+\n+  let binary = '';\n+  for (const byte of bytes) {\n+    binary += String.fromCharCode(byte);\n+  }\n+  return window.btoa(binary);\n+}\n+\n+export async function derivePasswordVerifier(\n+  password: string,\n+  passwordSalt: string,\n+): Promise<string> {\n+  const cryptoApi = globalThis.crypto?.subtle;\n+  if (!cryptoApi) {\n+    throw new Error('Web Crypto is unavailable');\n+  }\n+\n+  const passwordKey = await cryptoApi.importKey(\n+    'raw',\n+    new TextEncoder().encode(password),\n+    'PBKDF2',\n+    false,\n+    ['deriveBits'],\n+  );\n+\n+  const derivedBits = await cryptoApi.deriveBits(\n+    {\n+      name: 'PBKDF2',\n+      salt: decodeHex(passwordSalt),\n+      iterations: PASSWORD_VERIFIER_ITERATIONS,\n+      hash: 'SHA-256',\n+    },\n+    passwordKey,\n+    PASSWORD_VERIFIER_LENGTH * 8,\n+  );\n+\n+  return encodeBase64(new Uint8Array(derivedBits));\n+}\n@@ -13,7 +13,14 @@\n  */\n \n import type { User, UserStatus } from '@/types';\n-import { apiClient, setTokens, clearTokens, getAccessToken, getRefreshToken } from '@/lib/api-client';\n+import {\n+  apiClient,\n+  setTokens,\n+  clearTokens,\n+  getAccessToken,\n+  getRefreshToken,\n+} from '@/lib/api-client';\n+import { derivePasswordVerifier } from '@/lib/passwordAuth';\n \n // ─── Backend response shapes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\n \n@@ -22,6 +29,10 @@ interface AuthTokensResponse {\n   refreshToken: string;\n }\n \n+interface PasswordChallengeResponse {\n+  passwordSalt: string;\n+}\n+\n /** Shape returned by tRPC user.getCurrentUser (SELF_PROFILE_SELECT) */\n interface BackendUser {\n   id: string;\n@@ -84,7 +95,17 @@ export async function getCurrentUser(): Promise<User | null> {\n  * Stores the returned JWT tokens and returns the fetched user profile.\n  */\n export async function login(email: string, password: string): Promise<User> {\n-  const tokens = await apiClient.post<AuthTokensResponse>('/api/auth/login', { email, password });\n+  const { passwordSalt } = await apiClient.post<PasswordChallengeResponse>(\n+    '/api/auth/login/challenge',\n+    {\n+      email,\n+    },\n+  );\n+  const passwordVerifier = await derivePasswordVerifier(password, passwordSalt);\n+  const tokens = await apiClient.post<AuthTokensResponse>('/api/auth/login', {\n+    email,\n+    passwordVerifier,\n+  });\n   setTokens(tokens.accessToken, tokens.refreshToken);\n \n   const user = await apiClient.trpcQuery<BackendUser>('user.getCurrentUser');\n@@ -102,10 +123,15 @@ export async function register(\n   displayName: string,\n   password: string,\n ): Promise<User> {\n+  const { passwordSalt } = await apiClient.post<PasswordChallengeResponse>(\n+    '/api/auth/register/challenge',\n+  );\n+  const passwordVerifier = await derivePasswordVerifier(password, passwordSalt);\n   const tokens = await apiClient.post<AuthTokensResponse>('/api/auth/register', {\n     email,\n     username,\n-    password,\n+    passwordSalt,\n+    passwordVerifier,\n   });\n   setTokens(tokens.accessToken, tokens.refreshToken);\n \n@@ -169,4 +195,3 @@ export async function isAuthenticated(): Promise<boolean> {\n   const user = await getCurrentUser();\n   return user !== null;\n }\n-\n@@ -18,6 +18,12 @@ Shared knowledge base for the Harmony team. Add an entry whenever a mistake is c\n \n <!-- Most recent entries at the top -->\n \n+**Date:** 2026-04-11  \n+**Caught by:** [Human: user]  \n+**Related Issue:** #313  \n+**Mistake / Situation:** I began patching the auth implementation in the same pass as test creation instead of first proving the security regression with a failing test run.  \n+**Rule / Fix:** For every non-trivial Harmony code change, especially security work, do strict red-green-refactor: write or update the regression tests first, run them to capture the red state, and only then modify implementation.\n+\n **Date:** 2026-04-04  \n **Caught by:** [Human: @acabrera04]  \n **Related Issue:** N/A  ","comments":[{"url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334#issuecomment-4230319091","body":"[vc]: #GF9LQ1PjiE6Fm2x+tuJJ1gdvkhNjjJHoopW9Wz2kwSs=:\nThe latest updates on your projects. Learn more about [Vercel for GitHub](https://vercel.link/github-learn-more).\n\n| Project | Deployment | Actions | Updated (UTC) |\n| :--- | :----- | :------ | :------ |\n| [harmony](https://vercel.com/declans-projects-141a8b4c/harmony) | ![Ready](https://vercel.com/static/status/ready.svg) [Ready](https://vercel.com/declans-projects-141a8b4c/harmony/CAvEjUJ8qDcbsHPuLpEg6AVTX4LL) | [Preview](https://harmony-git-codex-issue-313-pa-fe2714-declans-projects-141a8b4c.vercel.app), [Comment](https://vercel.live/open-feedback/harmony-git-codex-issue-313-pa-fe2714-declans-projects-141a8b4c.vercel.app?via=pr-comment-feedback-link) | Apr 11, 2026 10:56pm |\n\n","user":{"login":"vercel[bot]","name":"vercel[bot]","email":null,"avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/8329?v=4","id":35613825},"id":4230319091,"pull_request_review_id":null,"in_reply_to_id":null,"created_at":"2026-04-11T22:49:04Z","review":null,"side":null,"line":null,"start_line":null,"path":null,"body_html":"<p dir=\"auto\">The latest updates on your projects. Learn more about <a href=\"https://vercel.link/github-learn-more\" rel=\"nofollow\">Vercel for GitHub</a>.</p>\n<markdown-accessiblity-table><table role=\"table\">\n<thead>\n<tr>\n<th align=\"left\">Project</th>\n<th align=\"left\">Deployment</th>\n<th align=\"left\">Actions</th>\n<th align=\"left\">Updated (UTC)</th>\n</tr>\n</thead>\n<tbody>\n<tr>\n<td align=\"left\"><a href=\"https://vercel.com/declans-projects-141a8b4c/harmony\" rel=\"nofollow\">harmony</a></td>\n<td align=\"left\"><a target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer nofollow\" href=\"https://camo.githubusercontent.com/2d058aa4937109084c3b00dc259081ef9f01257a8504a292a286041635575d96/68747470733a2f2f76657263656c2e636f6d2f7374617469632f7374617475732f72656164792e737667\"><img src=\"https://camo.githubusercontent.com/2d058aa4937109084c3b00dc259081ef9f01257a8504a292a286041635575d96/68747470733a2f2f76657263656c2e636f6d2f7374617469632f7374617475732f72656164792e737667\" alt=\"Ready\" data-canonical-src=\"https://vercel.com/static/status/ready.svg\" style=\"max-width: 100%;\"></a> <a href=\"https://vercel.com/declans-projects-141a8b4c/harmony/CAvEjUJ8qDcbsHPuLpEg6AVTX4LL\" rel=\"nofollow\">Ready</a></td>\n<td align=\"left\"><a href=\"https://harmony-git-codex-issue-313-pa-fe2714-declans-projects-141a8b4c.vercel.app\" rel=\"nofollow\">Preview</a>, <a href=\"https://vercel.live/open-feedback/harmony-git-codex-issue-313-pa-fe2714-declans-projects-141a8b4c.vercel.app?via=pr-comment-feedback-link\" rel=\"nofollow\">Comment</a></td>\n<td align=\"left\">Apr 11, 2026 10:56pm</td>\n</tr>\n</tbody>\n</table></markdown-accessiblity-table>"},{"url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334#pullrequestreview-4094321667","body":"## Code Review\n\n### Overview\n\nThis PR replaces raw password transport with a challenge/response pattern using client-side PBKDF2-SHA256 derivation. The flow is:\n\n1. Client fetches a random salt from `/register/challenge` or `/login/challenge`\n2. Client derives a verifier locally: `PBKDF2(password, salt, 310000, 32, SHA-256)`\n3. Client sends `{ passwordVerifier }` instead of `{ password }`\n4. Server stores credentials as `v1$<hex-salt>$<bcrypt(verifier)>`\n\nThis is a meaningful security improvement preventing raw passwords from appearing in request bodies, logs, or network captures.\n\n---\n\n### Code Quality & Correctness\n\n**Encoding/Decoding Logic — Correct**\nThe `decodePasswordVerifierRecord` function correctly handles the double-`$` ambiguity. Since bcrypt hashes start with `$2b$...`, the stored record looks like `v1$<32-hex>$$2b$12$...`. The `indexOf('$', prefix.length)` call correctly locates the single separator between salt and bcrypt hash.\n\n**PBKDF2 Parameters — Good**\n310,000 iterations, SHA-256, 32 bytes — consistent between frontend (Web Crypto API) and backend (Node crypto). Meets current NIST guidance.\n\n**Anti-enumeration — Good**\n`createDummyPasswordSalt(email)` returns a deterministic SHA-256-derived salt for unknown emails, preventing account enumeration via the challenge endpoint.\n\n**Versioned storage format — Good**\nThe `v1$` prefix makes future credential migrations tractable.\n\n---\n\n### Issues\n\n#### High\n\n**1. Significant test coverage regression unrelated to new contract**\n\nThe PR removes a large number of previously existing tests that have nothing to do with the password verifier change. Notable losses:\n\n- `refreshTokens`: 9 tests → 2. Lost: tampered token, wrong-secret JWT, expired token, token reuse prevention, expiry boundary, JWT claim verification, DB storage verification\n- `verifyAccessToken`: 6 tests → 2. Lost: tampered/wrong-secret/expired token rejection, no-DB-call invariant\n- `logout`: 4 tests → 1. Lost: idempotency, unknown-token safety, hash selectivity\n- `authService.register`: Lost `re-throws non-P2002 errors`, `joinServer soft-fail continues registration`, `displayName defaults to username`\n- `authService.login`: Lost admin upsert field correctness, admin OWNER assignment, error propagation tests\n\nThese tests cover token lifecycle logic this PR does not change — removing them is a net coverage regression that should be restored.\n\n**2. No migration path for existing deployed users**\n\nUsers with legacy bcrypt-only hashes (no `v1$` prefix) receive `UNAUTHORIZED` with a \"reset your password\" message, but there is no migration script or admin tooling to upgrade existing records. If any real user data exists in a deployed environment, those users will be locked out with no self-service path.\n\n#### Medium\n\n**3. Legacy hash path lacks timing equalization**\n\n```typescript\n// auth.service.ts login()\nconst decoded = decodePasswordVerifierRecord(user.passwordHash);\nif (!decoded) {\n  throw new TRPCError({ code: 'UNAUTHORIZED', ... });  // No bcrypt.compare called\n}\n```\n\nFor legacy-hash users, the code throws before calling `bcrypt.compare`, leaking timing information. An attacker can distinguish \"legacy account exists\" from \"account does not exist.\" Should call `await bcrypt.compare(passwordVerifier, TIMING_DUMMY_HASH)` before throwing.\n\n**4. Verifier replay attack surface not documented**\n\nThe scheme protects against raw password exposure in logs/request bodies, but a captured `passwordVerifier` can be directly replayed to `/api/auth/login`. This is weaker than SRP and the threat model should be documented (even a comment in the service) so future contributors understand why HTTPS is load-bearing for this scheme.\n\n#### Low\n\n**5. `passwordVerifierSchema` max length is loose**\n\n```typescript\nconst passwordVerifierSchema = z.string().min(20).max(128);\n```\n\nPBKDF2-SHA256 with 32 bytes encodes to exactly 44 base64 characters. A max of 128 is unnecessarily permissive. Tightening to `.length(44)` would make the contract self-documenting and reject garbage inputs earlier.\n\n---\n\n### Suggested Changes Before Merge\n\n1. **Restore removed tests** — particularly `refreshTokens`, `verifyAccessToken`, and `logout` edge cases. These don't interact with the verifier contract.\n2. **Add timing equalization for the legacy hash path** — call dummy bcrypt before throwing on `!decoded`.\n3. **Either provide a DB migration script** for existing users or explicitly document this is only valid for fresh deployments.\n4. **Optionally tighten** `passwordVerifierSchema` to `.length(44)`.","user":{"login":"AvanishKulkarni","name":"AvanishKulkarni","email":null,"avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/55012494?v=4","id":55012494},"id":4094321667,"pull_request_review_id":null,"in_reply_to_id":null,"created_at":"2026-04-11T22:59:00Z","review":"COMMENTED","side":null,"line":null,"start_line":null,"path":null,"body_html":"<h2 dir=\"auto\">Code Review</h2>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Overview</h3>\n<p dir=\"auto\">This PR replaces raw password transport with a challenge/response pattern using client-side PBKDF2-SHA256 derivation. The flow is:</p>\n<ol dir=\"auto\">\n<li>Client fetches a random salt from <code class=\"notranslate\">/register/challenge</code> or <code class=\"notranslate\">/login/challenge</code></li>\n<li>Client derives a verifier locally: <code class=\"notranslate\">PBKDF2(password, salt, 310000, 32, SHA-256)</code></li>\n<li>Client sends <code class=\"notranslate\">{ passwordVerifier }</code> instead of <code class=\"notranslate\">{ password }</code></li>\n<li>Server stores credentials as <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$&lt;hex-salt&gt;$&lt;bcrypt(verifier)&gt;</code></li>\n</ol>\n<p dir=\"auto\">This is a meaningful security improvement preventing raw passwords from appearing in request bodies, logs, or network captures.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Code Quality &amp; Correctness</h3>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>Encoding/Decoding Logic — Correct</strong><br>\nThe <code class=\"notranslate\">decodePasswordVerifierRecord</code> function correctly handles the double-<code class=\"notranslate\">$</code> ambiguity. Since bcrypt hashes start with <code class=\"notranslate\">$2b$...</code>, the stored record looks like <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$&lt;32-hex&gt;$$2b$12$...</code>. The <code class=\"notranslate\">indexOf('$', prefix.length)</code> call correctly locates the single separator between salt and bcrypt hash.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>PBKDF2 Parameters — Good</strong><br>\n310,000 iterations, SHA-256, 32 bytes — consistent between frontend (Web Crypto API) and backend (Node crypto). Meets current NIST guidance.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>Anti-enumeration — Good</strong><br>\n<code class=\"notranslate\">createDummyPasswordSalt(email)</code> returns a deterministic SHA-256-derived salt for unknown emails, preventing account enumeration via the challenge endpoint.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>Versioned storage format — Good</strong><br>\nThe <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$</code> prefix makes future credential migrations tractable.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Issues</h3>\n<h4 dir=\"auto\">High</h4>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>1. Significant test coverage regression unrelated to new contract</strong></p>\n<p dir=\"auto\">The PR removes a large number of previously existing tests that have nothing to do with the password verifier change. Notable losses:</p>\n<ul dir=\"auto\">\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">refreshTokens</code>: 9 tests → 2. Lost: tampered token, wrong-secret JWT, expired token, token reuse prevention, expiry boundary, JWT claim verification, DB storage verification</li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">verifyAccessToken</code>: 6 tests → 2. Lost: tampered/wrong-secret/expired token rejection, no-DB-call invariant</li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">logout</code>: 4 tests → 1. Lost: idempotency, unknown-token safety, hash selectivity</li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">authService.register</code>: Lost <code class=\"notranslate\">re-throws non-P2002 errors</code>, <code class=\"notranslate\">joinServer soft-fail continues registration</code>, <code class=\"notranslate\">displayName defaults to username</code></li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">authService.login</code>: Lost admin upsert field correctness, admin OWNER assignment, error propagation tests</li>\n</ul>\n<p dir=\"auto\">These tests cover token lifecycle logic this PR does not change — removing them is a net coverage regression that should be restored.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>2. No migration path for existing deployed users</strong></p>\n<p dir=\"auto\">Users with legacy bcrypt-only hashes (no <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$</code> prefix) receive <code class=\"notranslate\">UNAUTHORIZED</code> with a \"reset your password\" message, but there is no migration script or admin tooling to upgrade existing records. If any real user data exists in a deployed environment, those users will be locked out with no self-service path.</p>\n<h4 dir=\"auto\">Medium</h4>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>3. Legacy hash path lacks timing equalization</strong></p>\n<div class=\"highlight highlight-source-ts notranslate position-relative overflow-auto\" dir=\"auto\" data-snippet-clipboard-copy-content=\"// auth.service.ts login()\nconst decoded = decodePasswordVerifierRecord(user.passwordHash);\nif (!decoded) {\n  throw new TRPCError({ code: 'UNAUTHORIZED', ... });  // No bcrypt.compare called\n}\"><pre class=\"notranslate\"><span class=\"pl-c\">// auth.service.ts login()</span>\n<span class=\"pl-k\">const</span> <span class=\"pl-s1\">decoded</span> <span class=\"pl-c1\">=</span> <span class=\"pl-en\">decodePasswordVerifierRecord</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-s1\">user</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">passwordHash</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">;</span>\n<span class=\"pl-k\">if</span> <span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">!</span><span class=\"pl-s1\">decoded</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span> <span class=\"pl-kos\">{</span>\n  <span class=\"pl-k\">throw</span> <span class=\"pl-k\">new</span> <span class=\"pl-v\">TRPCError</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">{</span> <span class=\"pl-c1\">code</span>: <span class=\"pl-s\">'UNAUTHORIZED'</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">,</span> ... <span class=\"pl-kos\">}</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">;</span>  <span class=\"pl-c\">// No bcrypt.compare called</span>\n<span class=\"pl-kos\">}</span></pre></div>\n<p dir=\"auto\">For legacy-hash users, the code throws before calling <code class=\"notranslate\">bcrypt.compare</code>, leaking timing information. An attacker can distinguish \"legacy account exists\" from \"account does not exist.\" Should call <code class=\"notranslate\">await bcrypt.compare(passwordVerifier, TIMING_DUMMY_HASH)</code> before throwing.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>4. Verifier replay attack surface not documented</strong></p>\n<p dir=\"auto\">The scheme protects against raw password exposure in logs/request bodies, but a captured <code class=\"notranslate\">passwordVerifier</code> can be directly replayed to <code class=\"notranslate\">/api/auth/login</code>. This is weaker than SRP and the threat model should be documented (even a comment in the service) so future contributors understand why HTTPS is load-bearing for this scheme.</p>\n<h4 dir=\"auto\">Low</h4>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>5. <code class=\"notranslate\">passwordVerifierSchema</code> max length is loose</strong></p>\n<div class=\"highlight highlight-source-ts notranslate position-relative overflow-auto\" dir=\"auto\" data-snippet-clipboard-copy-content=\"const passwordVerifierSchema = z.string().min(20).max(128);\"><pre class=\"notranslate\"><span class=\"pl-k\">const</span> <span class=\"pl-s1\">passwordVerifierSchema</span> <span class=\"pl-c1\">=</span> <span class=\"pl-s1\">z</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-en\">string</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-en\">min</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">20</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-en\">max</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">128</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">;</span></pre></div>\n<p dir=\"auto\">PBKDF2-SHA256 with 32 bytes encodes to exactly 44 base64 characters. A max of 128 is unnecessarily permissive. Tightening to <code class=\"notranslate\">.length(44)</code> would make the contract self-documenting and reject garbage inputs earlier.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Suggested Changes Before Merge</h3>\n<ol dir=\"auto\">\n<li><strong>Restore removed tests</strong> — particularly <code class=\"notranslate\">refreshTokens</code>, <code class=\"notranslate\">verifyAccessToken</code>, and <code class=\"notranslate\">logout</code> edge cases. These don't interact with the verifier contract.</li>\n<li><strong>Add timing equalization for the legacy hash path</strong> — call dummy bcrypt before throwing on <code class=\"notranslate\">!decoded</code>.</li>\n<li><strong>Either provide a DB migration script</strong> for existing users or explicitly document this is only valid for fresh deployments.</li>\n<li><strong>Optionally tighten</strong> <code class=\"notranslate\">passwordVerifierSchema</code> to <code class=\"notranslate\">.length(44)</code>.</li>\n</ol>"}],"created_at":"2026-04-11T22:48:59Z","updated_at":"2026-04-11T22:59:00Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"commits":2,"changed_files":20,"additions":750,"deletions":610,"display_url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334","display_title":"Fix password transport with auth verifier flow"},"url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334","title":"Fix password transport with auth verifier flow","diff":"@@ -93,6 +93,13 @@ npx prisma migrate deploy\n npm test\n ```\n \n+If you seed the mock dataset, the loginable mock account is:\n+\n+```text\n+username/email: alice_admin / alice_admin@mock.harmony.test\n+password: HarmonyAdmin123!\n+```\n+\n If you want to run a single backend test file:\n \n ```bash\n@@ -14,8 +14,8 @@ This document provides the P4 specification for every backend module in the Harm\n \n | Capability | Description |\n |---|---|\n-| User registration | Creates a new account with email, username, and password. Validates email format, username (3–32 chars, alphanumeric + underscore/hyphen), and password (8–72 chars). |\n-| Login | Authenticates a user by email and password, returns an access token and a refresh token. Uses constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks. |\n+| User registration | Creates a new account with email, username, and a client-derived password verifier. The client first fetches a registration salt, derives a PBKDF2-SHA256 verifier locally, and sends `{ passwordSalt, passwordVerifier }` instead of the raw password. |\n+| Login | Authenticates a user by email and client-derived password verifier, returns an access token and a refresh token. The client first fetches the login salt, derives the verifier locally, and the backend uses constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks and enumeration. |\n | Token refresh | Rotates an existing refresh token into a new access/refresh pair. The old token is revoked atomically. |\n | Logout | Revokes a single refresh token. |\n | Rate limiting (planned) | Login: 10 attempts / 15 min. Registration: 5 attempts / hour (production). Not yet implemented — no rate-limiting middleware is present in `auth.router.ts`. |\n@@ -29,15 +29,16 @@ The Authentication module follows a stateless JWT pattern with server-side refre\n ```mermaid\n flowchart TD\n     Client -->|POST /api/auth/*| AuthRouter\n-    AuthRouter -->|validate body via Zod| AuthService\n-    AuthService -->|hash password / verify| Bcrypt\n+    AuthRouter -->|issue salt + validate body via Zod| AuthService\n+    Client -->|derive PBKDF2 verifier locally| Client\n+    AuthService -->|hash verifier / verify| Bcrypt\n     AuthService -->|sign / verify JWT| jsonwebtoken\n     AuthService -->|store / revoke refresh tokens| Prisma[(PostgreSQL)]\n     AuthService -->|return AuthTokens| AuthRouter\n     AuthRouter -->|JSON response| Client\n ```\n \n-**Design justification:** Stateless access tokens (short-lived, 15 min) keep the API horizontally scalable — no shared session store is required for read requests. Refresh tokens are stored hashed in PostgreSQL so that token revocation is authoritative and survives server restarts. bcrypt with 12 rounds provides adequate brute-force resistance without excessive CPU cost at current scale.\n+**Design justification:** Stateless access tokens (short-lived, 15 min) keep the API horizontally scalable — no shared session store is required for read requests. Refresh tokens are stored hashed in PostgreSQL so that token revocation is authoritative and survives server restarts. For credentials, the browser now derives a PBKDF2-SHA256 verifier from a server-issued salt before submission, and the backend bcrypt-hashes that verifier. This avoids sending raw passwords in request bodies while still preserving bcrypt-based storage and verification on the server.\n \n ### 3. Data Abstraction\n \n@@ -46,7 +47,7 @@ The module's core abstraction is the **AuthTokens** pair:\n - `accessToken` — a short-lived JWT (15 min) containing `{ sub: userId }`. Used by all other modules via the `requireAuth` middleware.\n - `refreshToken` — a long-lived JWT (7 days) containing `{ sub: userId, jti: uniqueId }`. Stored as a SHA-256 hash in the database.\n \n-Internally, the module treats password hashes as opaque strings produced and consumed only by bcrypt. No plaintext passwords are ever persisted.\n+Internally, the module stores password credentials as opaque verifier records with the shape `v1$<passwordSalt>$<bcrypt(passwordVerifier)>`. No plaintext passwords are persisted, and the raw password is no longer sent to `/api/auth/register` or `/api/auth/login`.\n \n ### 4. Stable Storage\n \n@@ -82,8 +83,10 @@ model User {\n \n | Method | Endpoint | Request Body | Response | Auth |\n |---|---|---|---|---|\n-| POST | `/api/auth/register` | `{ email, username, password }` email }` | `{ passwordSalt }` | None |\n+| POST | `/api/auth/login` | `{ email, passwordVerifier }` | `{ accessToken, refreshToken }` | None |\n | POST | `/api/auth/logout` | `{ refreshToken }` | `204 to cover the main success cases, all explicit error branches, and th\n \n ### 3.1 `register`\n \n-Purpose: create a new user account, hash the password with bcrypt, auto-join the default `harmony-hq` `harmony-hq`: `serverMemberService.joinServer` is called; if it fails, the error is caught and registration continues (soft fail).\n - Tokens are generated and stored: `signAccessToken` and fails, throws `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` with message \"Invalid credentials\".\n-- Login succeeds: user is found, password is valid, new tokens are generated and refresh token is stored.\n+- `TRPCError` with code `UNAUTHORIZED` and message instructing the account to reset its password before sign-in.\n+- Login succeeds: user is found, verifier is valid, new tokens are generated and refresh token is stored.\n - Tokens are issued and refresh token is persisted in DB.\n - Return value is `{ accessToken, refreshToken }`.\n \n+### 3.0 `generatePasswordSalt`\n+\n+Purpose: generate a new random hex salt for registration challenge responses.\n+\n+Program paths:\n+\n+- Success path: returns a 16-byte random salt encoded as 32 lowercase hexadecimal characters.\n+\n+### 3.0.1 `getLoginPasswordSalt`\n+\n+Purpose: return the stored password salt for an existing account, or a deterministic dummy salt for unknown emails to avoid account enumeration.\n+\n+Program paths:\n+\n+- Existing verifier record: extracts and returns the stored `passwordSalt`.\n+- Unknown user: returns a deterministic dummy salt derived from the email.\n+- Dev admin login in non-production: returns same 32-character hexadecimal string both times |\n+\n ## 5. Edge Cases to Explicitly Validate\n \n ### Security & Timing\n - **Timing attacks on login**: ensure `bcrypt.compare()` is ALWAYS called for non-existent emails, even though the user lookup fails early. Spy on bcrypt to verify this.\n - **No user enumeration**: both \"user not found\" and \"wrong password\" return identical error message and timing.\n+- **No raw password transport in service contract**: callers must derive and submit `passwordVerifier`; service tests should not pass raw passwords into `register` or `login`.\n - **JWT secret enforcement**: tokens signed with a different secret are rejected. Note: `jsonwebtoken` does not enforce an algorithm allowlist by default, so algorithm mismatch tests (e.g., HS512 vs HS256) may not fail with a shared HMAC secret.\n - **No plaintext password storage**: verify password is never logged, returned, or stored unencrypted.\n \n@@ -18,7 +18,7 @@ Each feature spec was authored independently and defines its own modules, classe\n | **Database** | PostgreSQL 16+ | ACID guarantees for visibility state transitions; native `ENUM` types for visibility; `JSONB` for flexible audit payloads; partial indexes for efficient public-channel queries. |\n | **Cache / EventBus** | Redis 7.2+ | Sub-millisecond reads for visibility checks on every public page load; Pub/Sub for cross-module event propagation (`VISIBILITY_CHANGED`, `MESSAGE_CREATED`, etc.) without tight coupling. |\n | **Authenticated APIs** | tRPC 11 | End-to-end type inference between Next.js client and Express server; eliminates hand-written API clients for admin operations. |\n-| **Public APIs** | REST (Express) | Search-engine crawlers, social-media link unfurlers, and external consumers require plain HTTP. tRPC's binary protocol is invisible to these consumers. |\n+| **Public APIs** | REST (Express) | Search-engine crawlers, social-media link unfurlers, and external consumers require plain HTTP. tRPC's binary protocol is invisible to these consumers. Authentication also uses REST challenge endpoints so the browser can fetch salts and submit password verifiers without sending raw passwords in request bodies. |\n | **Real-time Transport** | SSE (Server-Sent Events) | Native browser `EventSource` requires no client library; unidirectional server→client push fits message delivery perfectly; works over existing HTTP infrastructure with no WebSocket upgrade negotiation. |\n | **ORM** | Prisma 5.8+ | Type-safe schema definitions; auto-generated migrations; integrates with PostgreSQL enums. |\n | **Runtime Validation** | Zod 3.22+ | Composes with tRPC for automatic request/response validation; shared between client and server. |\n@@ -44,6 +44,8 @@ Each feature spec was authored independently and defines its own modules, classe\n \n **Why the split?** Crawlers (Googlebot, Bingbot) and social-media unfurlers (Facebook, Twitter/X, Slack) make standard HTTP requests. They cannot consume tRPC. Admin operations (visibility toggling, meta-tag overrides) benefit from tRPC's type inference and are only used by authenticated Harmony clients.\n \n+For authentication specifically, the REST surface also includes `/api/auth/register/challenge` and `/api/auth/login/challenge`. The browser uses these endpoints to fetch salts, derive a PBKDF2-SHA256 `passwordVerifier` locally, and then submit the verifier to `/api/auth/register` or `/api/auth/login` instead of the raw password.\n+\n ---\n \n ## 2. System Architecture Overview\n@@ -122,7 +124,7 @@ The `M-B`/`M-D` IDs are architectural groupings (by technical role). The detaile\n | *M-CV2* | *Public Channel Viewer* | *Client* | *Channel Visibility Toggle* | *Specified in [channel visibility spec](./dev-spec-channel-visibility-toggle.md)* | *—* |\n | *M-GV1* | *Public View (SSR)* | *Client* | *Guest Public Channel View* | *Specified in [guest public channel spec](./dev-spec-guest-public-channel-view.md)* | *—* |\n | *M-GV2* | *Client Interaction* | *Client* | *Guest Public Channel View* | *Specified in [guest public channel spec](./dev-spec-guest-public-channel-view.md)* | *—* |\n-| M-B1 | API Gateway | Server | Shared | tRPC router (authenticated) + REST controllers (public) | Cross-cutting infrastructure (all modules) |\n+| M-B1 | API Gateway | Server | Shared | tRPC router (authenticated) + REST controllers (public and auth challenge endpoints) | Cross-cutting infrastructure (all modules) |\n | M-B2 | Access Control | Server | Shared | Visibility guard, content filter, rate limiter, anonymous sessions | Module 1 (Auth middleware), Module 11 (Permission System) |\n | M-B3 | Visibility Management | Server | Channel Visibility Toggle | Visibility state machine, permission checks, audit logging | Module 3 (Channel Management — visibility ops) |\n | M-B4 | Content Delivery | Server | Guest Public Channel View | Message retrieval, author privacy, attachment processing | Module 9 (Public API & SEO — public delivery) |\n@@ -166,6 +166,11 @@ npx prisma migrate deploy\n # 4. createHash('sha1').update(`${MOCK_SEED_NAMESPACE}:${legacyId}`).digest();\n@@ -7,6 +7,20 @@ export const authRouter = Router();\n \n // ─── Input schemas ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n \n+const passwordSaltSchema = z\n+  .string()\n+  .length(32, { message: 'Password salt must be 32 hexadecimal characters' })\n+  .regex(/^[0-9a-f]+$/i, { message: 'Password salt must be hexadecimal' });\n+\n+const passwordVerifierSchema = z\n+  .string()\n+  .min(20, { message: 'Password verifier is required' })\n+  .max(128, { message: 'Password verifier is too long' });\n+\n+const challengeSchema = z.object({\n+  email: z.string().email({ message: 'Please enter a valid email address' }),\n+});\n+\n const registerSchema = expect(onJoined).toHaveBeenCalledWith('srv-123');\n@@ -0,0 +1,58 @@\n+const PASSWORD_VERIFIER_ITERATIONS = 310000;\n+const PASSWORD_VERIFIER_LENGTH = 32;\n+\n+function decodeHex(hex: string): ArrayBuffer {\n+  if (hex.length % 2 !== 0) {\n+    throw new Error('Invalid password salt');\n+  }\n+\n+  const bytes = new Uint8Array(hex.length / 2);\n+  for (let index = 0; index < hex.length; index += 2) {\n+    bytes[index / 2] = Number.parseInt(hex.slice(index, index + 2), 16);\n+  }\n+\n+  return bytes.buffer.slice(bytes.byteOffset, bytes.byteOffset + bytes.byteLength);\n+}\n+\n+function encodeBase64(bytes: Uint8Array): string {\n+  if (typeof window === 'undefined') {\n+    return Buffer.from(bytes).toString('base64');\n+  }\n+\n+  let binary = '';\n+  for (const byte of bytes) {\n+    binary += String.fromCharCode(byte);\n+  }\n+  return window.btoa(binary);\n+}\n+\n+export async function derivePasswordVerifier(\n+  password: string,\n+  passwordSalt: string,\n+): Promise<string> {\n+  const cryptoApi = globalThis.crypto?.subtle;\n+  if (!cryptoApi) {\n+    throw new Error('Web Crypto is unavailable');\n+  }\n+\n+  const passwordKey = await cryptoApi.importKey(\n+    'raw',\n+    new TextEncoder().encode(password),\n+    'PBKDF2',\n+    false,\n+    ['deriveBits'],\n+  );\n+\n+  const derivedBits = await cryptoApi.deriveBits(\n+    {\n+      name: 'PBKDF2',\n+      salt: decodeHex(passwordSalt),\n+      iterations: PASSWORD_VERIFIER_ITERATIONS,\n+      hash: 'SHA-256',\n+    },\n+    passwordKey,\n+    PASSWORD_VERIFIER_LENGTH * 8,\n+  );\n+\n+  return encodeBase64(new Uint8Array(derivedBits));\n+}\n@@ -13,7 +13,14 @@\n  */\n \n import type { User, UserStatus } from '@/types';\n-import { apiClient, setTokens, clearTokens, Add an entry whenever a mistake is c\n \n <!-- Most recent entries at the top -->\n \n+**Date:** 2026-04-11  \n+**Caught by:** [Human: user]  \n+**Related Issue:** #313  \n+**Mistake / Situation:** I began patching the auth implementation in the same pass as test creation instead of first proving the security regression with a failing test run.  \n+**Rule / Fix:** For every non-trivial Harmony code change, especially security work, do strict red-green-refactor: write or update the regression tests first, run them to capture the red state, and only then modify implementation.\n+\n **Date:** 2026-04-04  \n **Caught by:** [Human: @acabrera04]  \n **Related Issue:** N/A  ","comments":[{"url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334#issuecomment-4230319091","body":"[vc]: #GF9LQ1PjiE6Fm2x+tuJJ1gdvkhNjjJHoopW9Wz2kwSs=:\nThe latest updates on your projects. Learn more about <a href=\"https://vercel.link/github-learn-more\" rel=\"nofollow\">Vercel for GitHub</a>.</p>\n<markdown-accessiblity-table><table role=\"table\">\n<thead>\n<tr>\n<th align=\"left\">Project</th>\n<th align=\"left\">Deployment</th>\n<th align=\"left\">Actions</th>\n<th align=\"left\">Updated (UTC)</th>\n</tr>\n</thead>\n<tbody>\n<tr>\n<td align=\"left\"><a href=\"https://vercel.com/declans-projects-141a8b4c/harmony\" rel=\"nofollow\">harmony</a></td>\n<td align=\"left\"><a target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer nofollow\" href=\"https://camo.githubusercontent.com/2d058aa4937109084c3b00dc259081ef9f01257a8504a292a286041635575d96/68747470733a2f2f76657263656c2e636f6d2f7374617469632f7374617475732f72656164792e737667\"><img src=\"https://camo.githubusercontent.com/2d058aa4937109084c3b00dc259081ef9f01257a8504a292a286041635575d96/68747470733a2f2f76657263656c2e636f6d2f7374617469632f7374617475732f72656164792e737667\" alt=\"Ready\" data-canonical-src=\"https://vercel.com/static/status/ready.svg\" style=\"max-width: 100%;\"></a> <a href=\"https://vercel.com/declans-projects-141a8b4c/harmony/CAvEjUJ8qDcbsHPuLpEg6AVTX4LL\" rel=\"nofollow\">Ready</a></td>\n<td align=\"left\"><a href=\"https://harmony-git-codex-issue-313-pa-fe2714-declans-projects-141a8b4c.vercel.app\" rel=\"nofollow\">Preview</a>, <a href=\"https://vercel.live/open-feedback/harmony-git-codex-issue-313-pa-fe2714-declans-projects-141a8b4c.vercel.app?via=pr-comment-feedback-link\" rel=\"nofollow\">Comment</a></td>\n<td align=\"left\">Apr 11, 2026 10:56pm</td>\n</tr>\n</tbody>\n</table></markdown-accessiblity-table>"},{"url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334#pullrequestreview-4094321667","body":"## Code Review\n\n### Overview\n\nThis PR replaces raw password transport with a challenge/response pattern using client-side PBKDF2-SHA256 derivation. The flow is:\n\n1. Client fetches a random salt from `/register/challenge` or `/login/challenge`\n2. Client derives a verifier locally: `PBKDF2(password, salt, 310000, 32, SHA-256)`\n3. Client sends `{ passwordVerifier }` instead of `{ password }`\n4. Server stores credentials as `v1$<hex-salt>$<bcrypt(verifier)>`\n\nThis is a meaningful security improvement preventing raw passwords from appearing in request bodies, logs, or network captures.\n\n---\n\n### Code Quality & Correctness\n\n**Encoding/Decoding Logic — Correct**\nThe `decodePasswordVerifierRecord` function correctly handles the double-`$` ambiguity. Since bcrypt hashes start with `$2b$...`, the stored record looks like `v1$<32-hex>$$2b$12$...`. The `indexOf('$', prefix.length)` call correctly locates the single separator between salt and bcrypt hash.\n\n**PBKDF2 Parameters — Good**\n310,000 iterations, SHA-256, 32 bytes — consistent between frontend (Web Crypto API) and backend (Node crypto). Meets current NIST guidance.\n\n**Anti-enumeration — Good**\n`createDummyPasswordSalt(email)` returns a deterministic SHA-256-derived salt for unknown emails, preventing account enumeration via the challenge endpoint.\n\n**Versioned storage format — Good**\nThe `v1$` prefix makes future credential migrations tractable.\n\n---\n\n### Issues\n\n#### High\n\n**1. Significant test coverage regression unrelated to new contract**\n\nThe PR removes a large number of previously existing tests that have nothing to do with the password verifier change. Notable losses:\n\n- `refreshTokens`: 9 tests → 2. Lost: tampered token, wrong-secret JWT, expired token, token reuse prevention, expiry boundary, JWT claim verification, DB storage verification\n- `verifyAccessToken`: 6 tests → 2. Lost: tampered/wrong-secret/expired token rejection, no-DB-call invariant\n- `logout`: 4 tests → 1. Lost: idempotency, unknown-token safety, hash selectivity\n- `authService.register`: Lost `re-throws non-P2002 errors`, `joinServer soft-fail continues registration`, `displayName defaults to username`\n- `authService.login`: Lost admin upsert field correctness, admin OWNER assignment, error propagation tests\n\nThese tests cover token lifecycle logic this PR does not change — removing them is a net coverage regression that should be restored.\n\n**2. No migration path for existing deployed users**\n\nUsers with legacy bcrypt-only hashes (no `v1$` prefix) receive `UNAUTHORIZED` with a \"reset your password\" message, but there is no migration script or admin tooling to upgrade existing records. If any real user data exists in a deployed environment, those users will be locked out with no self-service path.\n\n#### Medium\n\n**3. Legacy hash path lacks timing equalization**\n\n```typescript\n// auth.service.ts login()\nconst decoded = decodePasswordVerifierRecord(user.passwordHash);\nif (!decoded) {\n  throw new TRPCError({ code: 'UNAUTHORIZED', ... });  // No bcrypt.compare called\n}\n```\n\nFor legacy-hash users, the code throws before calling `bcrypt.compare`, leaking timing information. An attacker can distinguish \"legacy account exists\" from \"account does not exist.\" Should call `await bcrypt.compare(passwordVerifier, TIMING_DUMMY_HASH)` before throwing.\n\n**4. Verifier replay attack surface not documented**\n\nThe scheme protects against raw password exposure in logs/request bodies, but a captured `passwordVerifier` can be directly replayed to `/api/auth/login`. This is weaker than SRP and the threat model should be documented (even a comment in the service) so future contributors understand why HTTPS is load-bearing for this scheme.\n\n#### Low\n\n**5. `passwordVerifierSchema` max length is loose**\n\n```typescript\nconst passwordVerifierSchema = z.string().min(20).max(128);\n```\n\nPBKDF2-SHA256 with 32 bytes encodes to exactly 44 base64 characters. A max of 128 is unnecessarily permissive. Tightening to `.length(44)` would make the contract self-documenting and reject garbage inputs earlier.\n\n---\n\n### Suggested Changes Before Merge\n\n1. **Restore removed tests** — particularly `refreshTokens`, `verifyAccessToken`, and `logout` edge cases. These don't interact with the verifier contract.\n2. **Add timing equalization for the legacy hash path** — call dummy bcrypt before throwing on `!decoded`.\n3. **Either provide a DB migration script** for existing users or explicitly document this is only valid for fresh deployments.\n4. **Optionally tighten** `passwordVerifierSchema` to `.length(44)`.","user":{"login":"AvanishKulkarni","name":"AvanishKulkarni","email":null,"avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/55012494?v=4","id":55012494},"id":4094321667,"pull_request_review_id":null,"in_reply_to_id":null,"created_at":"2026-04-11T22:59:00Z","review":"COMMENTED","side":null,"line":null,"start_line":null,"path":null,"body_html":"<h2 dir=\"auto\">Code Review</h2>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Overview</h3>\n<p dir=\"auto\">This PR replaces raw password transport with a challenge/response pattern using client-side PBKDF2-SHA256 derivation. The flow is:</p>\n<ol dir=\"auto\">\n<li>Client fetches a random salt from <code class=\"notranslate\">/register/challenge</code> or <code class=\"notranslate\">/login/challenge</code></li>\n<li>Client derives a verifier locally: <code class=\"notranslate\">PBKDF2(password, salt, 310000, 32, SHA-256)</code></li>\n<li>Client sends <code class=\"notranslate\">{ passwordVerifier }</code> instead of <code class=\"notranslate\">{ password }</code></li>\n<li>Server stores credentials as <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$&lt;hex-salt&gt;$&lt;bcrypt(verifier)&gt;</code></li>\n</ol>\n<p dir=\"auto\">This is a meaningful security improvement preventing raw passwords from appearing in request bodies, logs, or network captures.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Code Quality &amp; Correctness</h3>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>Encoding/Decoding Logic — Correct</strong><br>\nThe <code class=\"notranslate\">decodePasswordVerifierRecord</code> function correctly handles the double-<code class=\"notranslate\">$</code> ambiguity. Since bcrypt hashes start with <code class=\"notranslate\">$2b$...</code>, the stored record looks like <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$&lt;32-hex&gt;$$2b$12$...</code>. The <code class=\"notranslate\">indexOf('$', prefix.length)</code> call correctly locates the single separator between salt and bcrypt hash.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>PBKDF2 Parameters — Good</strong><br>\n310,000 iterations, SHA-256, 32 bytes — consistent between frontend (Web Crypto API) and backend (Node crypto). Meets current NIST guidance.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>Anti-enumeration — Good</strong><br>\n<code class=\"notranslate\">createDummyPasswordSalt(email)</code> returns a deterministic SHA-256-derived salt for unknown emails, preventing account enumeration via the challenge endpoint.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>Versioned storage format — Good</strong><br>\nThe <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$</code> prefix makes future credential migrations tractable.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Issues</h3>\n<h4 dir=\"auto\">High</h4>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>1. Significant test coverage regression unrelated to new contract</strong></p>\n<p dir=\"auto\">The PR removes a large number of previously existing tests that have nothing to do with the password verifier change. Notable losses:</p>\n<ul dir=\"auto\">\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">refreshTokens</code>: 9 tests → 2. Lost: tampered token, wrong-secret JWT, expired token, token reuse prevention, expiry boundary, JWT claim verification, DB storage verification</li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">verifyAccessToken</code>: 6 tests → 2. Lost: tampered/wrong-secret/expired token rejection, no-DB-call invariant</li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">logout</code>: 4 tests → 1. Lost: idempotency, unknown-token safety, hash selectivity</li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">authService.register</code>: Lost <code class=\"notranslate\">re-throws non-P2002 errors</code>, <code class=\"notranslate\">joinServer soft-fail continues registration</code>, <code class=\"notranslate\">displayName defaults to username</code></li>\n<li><code class=\"notranslate\">authService.login</code>: Lost admin upsert field correctness, admin OWNER assignment, error propagation tests</li>\n</ul>\n<p dir=\"auto\">These tests cover token lifecycle logic this PR does not change — removing them is a net coverage regression that should be restored.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>2. No migration path for existing deployed users</strong></p>\n<p dir=\"auto\">Users with legacy bcrypt-only hashes (no <code class=\"notranslate\">v1$</code> prefix) receive <code class=\"notranslate\">UNAUTHORIZED</code> with a \"reset your password\" message, but there is no migration script or admin tooling to upgrade existing records. If any real user data exists in a deployed environment, those users will be locked out with no self-service path.</p>\n<h4 dir=\"auto\">Medium</h4>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>3. Legacy hash path lacks timing equalization</strong></p>\n<div class=\"highlight highlight-source-ts notranslate position-relative overflow-auto\" dir=\"auto\" data-snippet-clipboard-copy-content=\"// auth.service.ts login()\nconst decoded = decodePasswordVerifierRecord(user.passwordHash);\nif (!decoded) {\n  throw new TRPCError({ code: 'UNAUTHORIZED', ... });  // No bcrypt.compare called\n}\"><pre class=\"notranslate\"><span class=\"pl-c\">// auth.service.ts login()</span>\n<span class=\"pl-k\">const</span> <span class=\"pl-s1\">decoded</span> <span class=\"pl-c1\">=</span> <span class=\"pl-en\">decodePasswordVerifierRecord</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-s1\">user</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">passwordHash</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">;</span>\n<span class=\"pl-k\">if</span> <span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">!</span><span class=\"pl-s1\">decoded</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span> <span class=\"pl-kos\">{</span>\n  <span class=\"pl-k\">throw</span> <span class=\"pl-k\">new</span> <span class=\"pl-v\">TRPCError</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">{</span> <span class=\"pl-c1\">code</span>: <span class=\"pl-s\">'UNAUTHORIZED'</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">,</span> ... <span class=\"pl-kos\">}</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">;</span>  <span class=\"pl-c\">// No bcrypt.compare called</span>\n<span class=\"pl-kos\">}</span></pre></div>\n<p dir=\"auto\">For legacy-hash users, the code throws before calling <code class=\"notranslate\">bcrypt.compare</code>, leaking timing information. An attacker can distinguish \"legacy account exists\" from \"account does not exist.\" Should call <code class=\"notranslate\">await bcrypt.compare(passwordVerifier, TIMING_DUMMY_HASH)</code> before throwing.</p>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>4. Verifier replay attack surface not documented</strong></p>\n<p dir=\"auto\">The scheme protects against raw password exposure in logs/request bodies, but a captured <code class=\"notranslate\">passwordVerifier</code> can be directly replayed to <code class=\"notranslate\">/api/auth/login</code>. This is weaker than SRP and the threat model should be documented (even a comment in the service) so future contributors understand why HTTPS is load-bearing for this scheme.</p>\n<h4 dir=\"auto\">Low</h4>\n<p dir=\"auto\"><strong>5. <code class=\"notranslate\">passwordVerifierSchema</code> max length is loose</strong></p>\n<div class=\"highlight highlight-source-ts notranslate position-relative overflow-auto\" dir=\"auto\" data-snippet-clipboard-copy-content=\"const passwordVerifierSchema = z.string().min(20).max(128);\"><pre class=\"notranslate\"><span class=\"pl-k\">const</span> <span class=\"pl-s1\">passwordVerifierSchema</span> <span class=\"pl-c1\">=</span> <span class=\"pl-s1\">z</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-en\">string</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-en\">min</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">20</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">.</span><span class=\"pl-en\">max</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">(</span><span class=\"pl-c1\">128</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">)</span><span class=\"pl-kos\">;</span></pre></div>\n<p dir=\"auto\">PBKDF2-SHA256 with 32 bytes encodes to exactly 44 base64 characters. A max of 128 is unnecessarily permissive. Tightening to <code class=\"notranslate\">.length(44)</code> would make the contract self-documenting and reject garbage inputs earlier.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3 dir=\"auto\">Suggested Changes Before Merge</h3>\n<ol dir=\"auto\">\n<li><strong>Restore removed tests</strong> — particularly <code class=\"notranslate\">refreshTokens</code>, <code class=\"notranslate\">verifyAccessToken</code>, and <code class=\"notranslate\">logout</code> edge cases. These don't interact with the verifier contract.</li>\n<li><strong>Add timing equalization for the legacy hash path</strong> — call dummy bcrypt before throwing on <code class=\"notranslate\">!decoded</code>.</li>\n<li><strong>Either provide a DB migration script</strong> for existing users or explicitly document this is only valid for fresh deployments.</li>\n<li><strong>Optionally tighten</strong> <code class=\"notranslate\">passwordVerifierSchema</code> to <code class=\"notranslate\">.length(44)</code>.</li>\n</ol>"}],"display_url":"https://github.com/CS485-Harmony/Harmony/pull/334","display_title":"Fix password transport with auth verifier flow"}

#### 🤖 gpt-5.4 (medium)

- Time: 2026-04-11T23:00:50.199Z

The reviewer is right on that point. I removed unrelated auth coverage when I rewrote the auth service tests around the verifier contract; that was a regression, not intentional scope control. I’m restoring the lost token-lifecycle tests and addressing the other actionable review items in the same pass.

#### Tool: exec_command

```sh
git show origin/main:harmony-backend/tests/auth.service.test.ts | sed -n '1,260p'
```

Output:

```text
/**
 * Auth service unit tests — Issue #258
 *
 * Covers authService.register, login, logout, refreshTokens, verifyAccessToken.
 * Prisma and serverMemberService are fully mocked — no real DB required.
 *
 * IMPORTANT: JWT secrets are read at module import time. Tests use the
 * module re-import pattern (jest.resetModules + jest.isolateModules) whenever
 * env vars need to differ between tests (e.g. production mode).
 */

// ─── Set env vars before any imports ─────────────────────────────────────────

process.env.JWT_ACCESS_SECRET = 'test-access-secret';
process.env.JWT_REFRESH_SECRET = 'test-refresh-secret';
process.env.JWT_ACCESS_EXPIRES_IN = '15m';
process.env.JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRES_DAYS = '7';
process.env.NODE_ENV = 'test';

// ─── Mocks ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jest.mock('../src/db/prisma', () => ({
  prisma: {
    user: {
      findUnique: jest.fn(),
      create: jest.fn(),
      upsert: jest.fn(),
    },
    refreshToken: {
      create: jest.fn(),
      updateMany: jest.fn(),
    },
    server: {
      findFirst: jest.fn(),
      findMany: jest.fn(),
    },
    serverMember: {
      upsert: jest.fn(),
    },
  },
}));

jest.mock('../src/services/serverMember.service', () => ({
  serverMemberService: { joinServer: jest.fn() },
}));

// ─── Imports ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';
import bcrypt from 'bcryptjs';
import crypto from 'crypto';
import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';
import { prisma } from '../src/db/prisma';
import { serverMemberService } from '../src/services/serverMember.service';
import { authService } from '../src/services/auth.service';

// ─── Typed mock helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const mockPrisma = prisma as unknown as {
  user: {
    findUnique: jest.Mock;
    create: jest.Mock;
    upsert: jest.Mock;
  };
  refreshToken: {
    create: jest.Mock;
    updateMany: jest.Mock;
  };
  server: {
    findFirst: jest.Mock;
    findMany: jest.Mock;
  };
  serverMember: {
    upsert: jest.Mock;
  };
};

const mockJoinServer = serverMemberService.joinServer as jest.Mock;

// ─── Shared fixtures ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

const ACCESS_SECRET = 'test-access-secret';
const REFRESH_SECRET = 'test-refresh-secret';
const ADMIN_EMAIL = 'admin@harmony.dev';

const mockUserId = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001';
const mockUser = {
  id: mockUserId,
  email: 'user@example.com',
  username: 'testuser',
  passwordHash: '', // set in beforeAll
  displayName: 'testuser',
  avatarUrl: null,
  publicProfile: true,
  createdAt: new Date(),
};

const mockRefreshTokenRecord = {
  id: '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002',
  tokenHash: '',
  userId: mockUserId,
  expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000),
  revokedAt: null,
  createdAt: new Date(),
};

// ─── Utility: sign a real refresh token with the test secret ─────────────────

function signTestRefreshToken(userId: string, overrides?: jwt.SignOptions): string {
  return jwt.sign(
    { sub: userId, jti: crypto.randomUUID() },
    REFRESH_SECRET,
    { expiresIn: '7d', ...overrides },
  );
}

function signTestAccessToken(userId: string, overrides?: jwt.SignOptions): string {
  return jwt.sign({ sub: userId }, ACCESS_SECRET, { expiresIn: '15m', ...overrides });
}

// ─── Global setup ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

beforeAll(async () => {
  mockUser.passwordHash = await bcrypt.hash('SecurePass123!', 4); // fast for tests
});

beforeEach(() => {
  // resetAllMocks clears mock call history AND the Once queue so that
  // unconsumed mockResolvedValueOnce values from a previous test do not
  // bleed into the next one.
  jest.resetAllMocks();
  // Default stubs — override per test as needed
  mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
  mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);
  mockPrisma.user.upsert.mockResolvedValue({ ...mockUser, email: ADMIN_EMAIL, username: 'admin' });
  mockPrisma.refreshToken.create.mockResolvedValue(mockRefreshTokenRecord);
  mockPrisma.refreshToken.updateMany.mockResolvedValue({ count: 1 });
  mockPrisma.server.findFirst.mockResolvedValue(null);
  mockPrisma.server.findMany.mockResolvedValue([]);
  mockPrisma.serverMember.upsert.mockResolvedValue({});
  mockJoinServer.mockResolvedValue(undefined);
});

// ─── 4.1 register ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

describe('authService.register', () => {
  it('returns tokens when registering with valid credentials', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);

    const result = await authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!');

    expect(typeof result.accessToken).toBe('string');
    expect(result.accessToken.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
    expect(typeof result.refreshToken).toBe('string');
    expect(result.refreshToken.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
  });

  it('stores the refresh token hash in the database with correct expiry', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);

    const before = Date.now();
    await authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!');
    const after = Date.now();

    expect(mockPrisma.refreshToken.create).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
    const createArg = mockPrisma.refreshToken.create.mock.calls[0][0] as {
      data: { tokenHash: string; userId: string; expiresAt: Date };
    };
    expect(createArg.data.userId).toBe(mockUserId);
    expect(typeof createArg.data.tokenHash).toBe('string');
    expect(createArg.data.tokenHash).toHaveLength(64); // sha256 hex
    // Verify expiresAt is ~7 calendar days in the future using the same
    // Date#setDate logic as the implementation to avoid DST-related flakiness.
    const expiresMs = createArg.data.expiresAt.getTime();
    const expectedMin = new Date(before);
    expectedMin.setDate(expectedMin.getDate() + 7);
    const expectedMax = new Date(after);
    expectedMax.setDate(expectedMax.getDate() + 7);
    expect(expiresMs).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(expectedMin.getTime());
    expect(expiresMs).toBeLessThanOrEqual(expectedMax.getTime());
  });

  it('rejects duplicate email with CONFLICT', async () => {
    // First findUnique (email check) returns existing user → throws before username check
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValueOnce(mockUser);

    await expect(
      authService.register('user@example.com', 'newuser', 'pass'),
    ).rejects.toMatchObject({ code: 'CONFLICT', message: 'Email already in use' });
  });

  it('rejects duplicate username with CONFLICT', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique
      .mockResolvedValueOnce(null) // email check: not taken
      .mockResolvedValueOnce(mockUser); // username check: taken

    await expect(
      authService.register('new@example.com', 'testuser', 'pass'),
    ).rejects.toMatchObject({ code: 'CONFLICT', message: 'Username already taken' });
  });

  it('maps Prisma P2002 race condition to CONFLICT', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    const p2002 = new Prisma.PrismaClientKnownRequestError('Unique constraint failed', {
      code: 'P2002',
      clientVersion: '5.0.0',
    });
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockRejectedValue(p2002);

    await expect(
      authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!'),
    ).rejects.toMatchObject({ code: 'CONFLICT', message: 'Email or username already in use' });
  });

  it('re-throws non-P2002 Prisma errors', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    const unknownError = new Error('DB connection lost');
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockRejectedValue(unknownError);

    await expect(
      authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!'),
    ).rejects.toThrow('DB connection lost');
  });

  it('calls joinServer when default server exists', async () => {
    const defaultServerId = 'server-id-001';
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);
    mockPrisma.server.findFirst.mockResolvedValue({ id: defaultServerId });

    await authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!');

    expect(mockJoinServer).toHaveBeenCalledWith(mockUserId, defaultServerId);
  });

  it('succeeds without calling joinServer when no default server exists', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);
    mockPrisma.server.findFirst.mockResolvedValue(null);

    const result = await authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!');

    expect(result.accessToken).toBeTruthy();
    expect(mockJoinServer).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
  });

  it('continues registration when joinServer fails (soft fail)', async () => {
    mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
    mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);
    mockPrisma.server.findFirst.mockResolvedValue({ id: 'server-001' });
    mockJoinServer.mockRejectedValue(new Error('Server join failed'));

    // Should NOT throw — soft fail
    const result = await authService.register('user@example.com', 'testuser', 'SecurePass123!');

    expect(result.accessToken).toBeTruthy();
    expect(result.refreshToken).toBeTruthy();
  });
```

```text
/**
 * Auth service unit tests — Issues #258 / #313
 */

process.env.JWT_ACCESS_SECRET = 'test-access-secret';
process.env.JWT_REFRESH_SECRET = 'test-refresh-secret';

process.env.NODE_ENV = 'test';

import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';
import bcrypt from 'bcryptjs';
import crypto from 'crypto';
import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';
import { prisma } from '../src/db/prisma';
import { serverMemberService } from '../src/services/serverMember.service';
import { authService } from '../src/services/auth.service';

const PASSWORD_SALT = '00112233445566778899aabbccddeeff';
const DEV_ADMIN_SALT = 'f6f0e4f9f5f841caa4dd4ac4ef0bf9e8';

function derivePasswordVerifier(password: string, passwordSalt = PASSWORD_SALT): string {
  return crypto.pbkdf2Sync(password, passwordSalt, 310000, 32, 'sha256').toString('base64');
}

    findUnique: jest.Mock;
    create: jest.Mock;

const ACCESS_SECRET = 'test-access-secret';
const REFRESH_SECRET = 'test-refresh-secret';

const mockUserId = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001';
const mockUser = {
  id: mockUserId,
  email: 'user@example.com',
  username: 'testuser',

  displayName: 'testuser',
  avatarUrl: null,
  publicProfile: true,
  createdAt: new Date(),
};

const mockRefreshTokenRecord = {
  id: '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002',
  tokenHash: '',
  userId: mockUserId,
  expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000),
  revokedAt: null,
  createdAt: new Date(),
};

function signTestRefreshToken(userId: string, overrides?: jwt.SignOptions): string {

    expiresIn: '7d',
    ...overrides,
  });
}

function signTestAccessToken(userId: string, overrides?: jwt.SignOptions): string {
  return jwt.sign({ sub: userId }, ACCESS_SECRET, { expiresIn: '15m', ...overrides });
}

beforeAll(async () => {
  mockUser.passwordHash = `v1$${PASSWORD_SALT}$${await bcrypt.hash(derivePasswordVerifier('SecurePass123!'), 4)}`;
});

beforeEach(() => {
  jest.resetAllMocks();
  mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(null);
  mockPrisma.user.create.mockResolvedValue(mockUser);
  mockPrisma.user.upsert.mockResolvedValue({

    username: 'admin',

  mockPrisma.refreshToken.create.mockResolvedValue(mockRefreshTokenRecord);
  mockPrisma.refreshToken.updateMany.mockResolvedValue({ count: 1 });
```
